For perspective, this same expanded group represented just 29% of the S&P500 at the peak of the dotcom mania.

The community is buzzing about high-bandwidth memory (HBM) demand, with Stocktwits messages warning short sellers and predicting a return to all-time highs. Other notable discussions include the space sector, where AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) jumped from rank 46 to 5, and Rocket Lab (RKLB) also saw a surge. There is clear disagreement on Tesla (TSLA), where mentions rose sharply but Stocktwits sentiment is split 11 bullish vs 13 bearish. Similarly, the broad market via SPY is flat, with traders noting it's 'sitting here until further notice,' indicating uncertainty about the next direction.

overlooked_insights.title: HBM wafer reallocation tightening supply
overlooked_insights.insight: Users report that each memory manufacturer is independently shifting wafer and packaging capacity toward HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) because customers are demanding significantly more. This internal reallocation could tighten supply of other memory types (DRAM, NAND) and support pricing, contrary to the headline narrative that only external competition from SK Hynix/Samsung poses a threat.
overlooked_insights.evidence: User $MU $SNDK states: 'Customers have been saying they want significantly more HBM. Each manufacturer has an independent reason to allocate more wafers and packaging capacity toward HBM.'
overlooked_insights.why_overlooked: Mainstream headlines focus on competitive threats rather than the supply-side dynamics of capacity shifting within the industry.

overlooked_insights.title: Apple's Chinese CXMT memory sourcing unlikely to ease shortage
overlooked_insights.insight: Users highlight a Nikkei article (linked by one user) noting that Apple is lobbying to secure memory chips from Chinese supplier CXMT, but analysts say even if successful, it won't resolve the global supply-demand imbalance because CXMT's capacity is far below domestic demand. This counters the price-gouging narrative in headlines by pointing to structural undersupply.
overlooked_insights.evidence: User $SNDK quotes: 'CXMT's prospectus notes that the company's capacity is far below domestic demand; combined with ongoing global memory supply-demand imbalances, even if Apple's lobbying succeeds ... it won't fundamentally change the situation.'
overlooked_insights.why_overlooked: Mainstream coverage like Investor's Business Daily asks if memory-chip makers are price-gouging, but does not discuss Apple's specific sourcing efforts or the China supply chain angle.

overlooked_insights.title: Microsoft datacenter grid constraints and potential sale
overlooked_insights.insight: One user describes Microsoft's datacenter ecosystem facing 'increasing difficulties to develop and connect to the grid,' and suggests the exit strategy is to sell the infrastructure business at a low discount rate. This implies a possible spin-off or divestiture that is not covered in mainstream headlines about AI models or quantum computing.
overlooked_insights.evidence: User $MSFT states: 'building on infrastructure looks quite solid in a datacenter ecosystem with increasingly difficulties to develop and connect to the grid. And the exit strategy still looks great: sell an infrastructure business.'
overlooked_insights.why_overlooked: Mainstream articles on Microsoft focus on product launches, partnerships, and general AI opportunities, ignoring operational bottlenecks and strategic exit options.

overlooked_insights.title: Wendy's borrow rate and float tightening for squeeze
overlooked_insights.insight: Users provide specific numbers: ~50 million shares to cover, a 30% borrow rate, and a cost-to-borrow (CTB) nearly equal to the short interest percentage. They argue that high borrowing costs prevent shorts from flooding sell orders, and the float is tightening hourly, setting up a squeeze. Mainstream coverage mentions the meme surge but lacks these operational details.
overlooked_insights.evidence: User $WEN says: 'let's say it's around 50mm shares to cover ..... 30% borrow rate so they can't flood the sells anymore, .... float getting tighter and tighter by the hour..... Kaboom.' Another user shares a link to real-time cost-to-borrow data.
overlooked_insights.why_overlooked: Headlines such as Motley Fool's 'What Investors Should Know About Wendy's Sudden Surge' talk about yield and P/E, not the specific short squeeze mechanics retail traders are monitoring.

overlooked_insights.title: ASTS Japan Starlink subsidies and institutional short theory

Additionally, a user claims that Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, JP Morgan, and Jefferies facilitated massive short positions across the space sector during the SpaceX IPO, and that the Iridium acquisition and ASTS J-LEO report brought institutional covering. This specific short narrative is absent from mainstream coverage.

They also cite price targets ranging from $85 to $250 post-Phase 3 readout (the '80th event'), implying huge upside if successful. ASTS.summary: ASTS is on fire in online communities right now, with a massive spike in Reddit mentions from 12 to 148 in just 24 hours, ranking it 5th overall. On Stocktwits, bullish tags outnumber bearish 31 to 4, showing overwhelming positive sentiment. The buzz centers around a few key themes: First, a YouTube video (likely a bullish analysis) is being shared. Second, there's a narrative that big banks like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley are shorting space stocks ahead of a SpaceX IPO, and that ASTS is one of the stocks that could squeeze shorts. The stock is seen as a pure play on satellite-to-phone direct connectivity, a disruptive technology that could revolutionize telecom. The main risks are execution, competition from Starlink, and the possibility that the short squeeze narrative doesn't materialize. Given the rapid price moves, investors should have a high risk tolerance.

OUST.summary: Ouster (OUST) is getting a lot of attention on Stocktwits and Reddit right now, mainly because of a massive price surge. The stock jumped over 24% in a single day, and some traders are calling it a 'god candle'—a huge green candle that signals strong momentum. People are excited but also cautious. Many are talking about taking profits, with one user saying they locked in gains at $52. Others are wondering if it can reach $100 soon, though that seems like a stretch. The general vibe is that this move is driven by momentum and possibly a short squeeze, not necessarily fundamentals. Some traders are watching for a pullback to support levels around $49 to see if the trend holds. There's also chatter about other stocks like IBM and KOPN, but OUST is the star performer. The sentiment is overwhelmingly bullish, with 18 bullish tags vs only 1 bearish on Stocktwits. However, the lack of Reddit mentions suggests this is more of a Stocktwits-driven hype. Investors should know that Ouster is a lidar sensor company for autonomous vehicles, which is a high-growth but volatile sector. The recent surge could be tied to broader market rotation into high-beta names or specific company news, but the messages don't mention any catalysts. The risk is that this could be a short-lived spike, and those buying at the top might get burned. Overall, the community is excited but wary, and many are taking profits rather than adding new positions.
